walkthrough.title: Build the Sea Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Century: Eastern Wonders set up. First, take the sea tiles and arrange them into the modular board. For your first game, use the suggested layout from the rulebook. Place the tiles so each hex connects by at least one edge to another tile. The board should form a roughly rectangular shape with some open water gaps between the islands.

walkthrough.title: Place Market Tiles on Islands
walkthrough.text: Now find the market tiles. These are the small square tiles showing spice trade conversions. Shuffle them face down and place one on each island that has a market space. Each market tile shows what spices you give and what you get in return. Leave the four corner port spaces empty for now.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Port Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the point cards, which are sometimes called port cards. Shuffle them into a face-down deck. Deal four cards face up in a row next to the board. Place the matching bonus tokens above the first and second cards in the row. These are the bonus tokens worth three and one extra points. Whenever someone claims one of those cards, they also take the bonus token.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Spice Supply
walkthrough.text: Set out all the spice cubes where everyone can reach them. There are four types, and their value goes from lowest to highest: yellow, red, green, and brown. Think of it as a ladder. Yellow is the most common and least valuable. Brown is the rarest and most valuable. Put them all in a central supply area.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Components
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a player board, a boat token, and all the trading post tokens of their color. Place the trading posts on your player board in the marked spaces. You will pop these off as you build them on the map. Each player also takes a cargo hold card which can store up to ten spice cubes total.

walkthrough.title: Starting Spices and Boat Placement
walkthrough.text: Each player receives starting spices based on turn order. The first player gets four yellow cubes. The second player gets four yellow cubes. The third player gets three yellow and one red. The fourth player gets three yellow and one red. Place your boat on any sea tile that borders the edge of the board.

walkthrough.title: Place the Harvest Markers
walkthrough.text: Find the four harvest tiles marked with the yellow spice icon. Place one on each of the four designated harvest islands around the edges of the board. These are the islands where players can pick up two yellow spices instead of trading. They are important early-game stops for restocking your supply.

walkthrough.title: Select First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a random player to go first and give them the first player marker. Play goes clockwise from the first player. You are all set up and ready to sail. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how a turn works in Century: Eastern Wonders. On your turn, you do two things in order. First, you move your boat. Then you take one action at your destination island. That's it. Move and act. The game flows quickly because turns are simple, but the decisions stack up fast.

walkthrough.title: Moving Your Boat
walkthrough.text: Your boat can move one sea tile for free. If you want to move further, each additional tile costs one spice cube of any type. Drop the spice onto the tile you are leaving, not the one you are entering. Other players can pick up dropped spices when they pass through those tiles later. So think carefully about how far you move and what you leave behind for your opponents.

walkthrough.title: Picking Up Dropped Spices
walkthrough.text: When you move through a sea tile that has spices dropped on it by other players, you pick up all those spices for free. This does not cost extra movement. You simply scoop them up as your boat passes through. This is a nice little bonus and can sometimes make a longer route worthwhile if someone left good spices along the way.

walkthrough.title: Action: Market
walkthrough.text: If you stop on an island with a market tile, you can trade spices. The market tile shows a conversion, like give two yellow and get one green. You may perform this conversion as many times as you want on a single visit, as long as you have the spices to pay. But there is a catch. You must have a trading post on that island to use it. If you do not have one, you need to build one first.

walkthrough.title: Action: Build a Trading Post
walkthrough.text: Instead of trading at a market island, you can build a trading post there. Take the leftmost available trading post from your player board and place it on the island. The cost depends on how many posts you have already built. Your first few posts are free or cheap, but later ones cost spices to place. The spice type required depends on the island's terrain type shown on the tile.

walkthrough.title: Trading Post Costs
walkthrough.text: Let's break down the trading post costs. Your player board has rows and columns of trading post slots. As you remove posts from a column, the cost increases. The first post in each column is free. The second costs one spice matching the island terrain. The third costs two spices, and so on. Different columns represent different terrain types. Planning which posts to build and in what order is a key part of the strategy.

walkthrough.title: Player Board Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Here is a really important detail that new players sometimes miss. When you remove trading posts from your player board in certain patterns, you unlock permanent bonuses. These bonuses might give you extra spices, free upgrades, or other advantages that trigger for the rest of the game. Always check your player board when building a post to see if you have revealed a new bonus.

walkthrough.title: Action: Port
walkthrough.text: If you stop on a port island, you can fulfill one of the face-up point cards. Each point card shows a specific combination of spices required. Pay those spices from your cargo back to the supply and take the card. It is worth the victory points printed on it. If the card was in the first or second position and a bonus token is still there, you also take that bonus token. Then slide the remaining cards to the left and deal a new card to fill the empty spot on the right.

walkthrough.title: Action: Harvest
walkthrough.text: If you stop on a harvest island, you collect two yellow spice cubes from the supply and add them to your cargo. That is it. No trading post needed, no cost. Harvesting is simple and gives you raw materials to start trading up. It is especially useful early in the game when you need spices to fuel your first few trades.

walkthrough.title: Cargo Limit
walkthrough.text: Your cargo hold can store a maximum of ten spice cubes at any time. If you would gain spices that put you over ten, you simply do not take the extras. This means you cannot hoard endlessly. You need to spend spices by trading up or fulfilling point cards to make room for new ones. Keep an eye on your cargo count before you harvest or trade.

walkthrough.title: Game End Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game end is triggered when any player claims their fourth point card. When that happens, finish the current round so that every player has had the same number of turns. Then move to final scoring. Four cards is the target, but you are not required to stop there if the round is still going.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Add up your final score from three sources. First, add the victory points printed on all your point cards. Second, add any bonus tokens you collected. Third, count one point for each non-yellow spice remaining in your cargo. Red, green, and brown cubes are each worth one point. Yellow cubes are worth nothing. The player with the highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most spice cubes remaining wins.

summary.title: Turn Overview
bullets: Each turn: Move boat → Take 1 action at destination
Actions: Market, Build Trading Post, Port, or Harvest

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Century: Eastern Wonders! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with four players. Player one through Player four will each take different approaches so you can see how the game flows. Follow along with the moves even if you have fewer people at the table. Ready to set sail? Let's go.

walkthrough.title: Starting Positions
walkthrough.text: Everyone has placed their boats on edge sea tiles. Players one and two each have four yellow spices. Players three and four each have three yellow and one red. The board is laid out with market islands, harvest islands, and port islands. Four point cards are face up with bonus tokens on the first two.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Harvest for Spices
walkthrough.text: Player one moves one tile for free to a nearby harvest island. They collect two yellow spices from the supply, bringing their total to six yellow. A nice safe opening. Harvesting is a great first move because it stocks you up with raw materials for trading later.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Build a Trading Post
walkthrough.text: Player two moves one tile to a market island that converts yellow spices into red. They build their first trading post there. The first post is free, so it costs nothing extra. Now Player two has permanent access to this market for the rest of the game. Smart move to set up early.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: The First Mistake — Moving Too Far
walkthrough.text: Player three spots a great market island three tiles away. They move their boat three tiles, paying two spices for the extra movement. That is two yellow spices dropped on tiles along the way, leaving only one yellow and one red in cargo. They arrive and realize they barely have anything left to trade with. The spices they dropped are now sitting on the board for opponents to scoop up.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch Your Movement Costs
walkthrough.text: Moving far in one turn feels efficient, but it drains your spices fast. Every extra tile beyond the first costs one spice, and those dropped spices become free gifts for opponents sailing through. Early in the game, stay close to nearby islands and build a network of trading posts within reach. Plan routes that are one or two tiles apart, not three or four.

walkthrough.title: Player 4: Build Near a Port
walkthrough.text: Player four moves one tile to a market island near one of the port islands. They build a trading post for free. Player four is already thinking ahead about which point card they want to fulfill. Building near a port means fewer movement costs when it is time to deliver spices for points.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Trade at a Market
walkthrough.text: Player one moves to the market island where they just built a post last turn. Wait, Player one did not build a post yet. They went to a harvest island first. So this turn, Player one moves to a market island and builds their first trading post. Now they are set up to trade here next turn. Sometimes it takes two turns to get a market running, and that is perfectly fine.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: First Trade
walkthrough.text: Player two stays on their market island and uses it. The market converts two yellow into one red. Player two does this twice, turning four yellow into two red. Now they have two red spices ready to use. The conversion engine is starting to hum.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: The Second Mistake — No Trading Posts
walkthrough.text: Player three finally has some spices again after harvesting. They sail to a market island that converts red into green, which is exactly what they need. But they never built a trading post here. They cannot use this market without one. Player three has to spend their action building a post instead of trading. That is an entire turn lost because they did not plan ahead.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Posts Before You Need Them
walkthrough.text: Trading posts are the foundation of your strategy. You cannot use a market island without one. Plan your route a few turns ahead and build posts on the islands you will need before you need them. A good approach is to build two or three posts in your first few turns, then start trading. Do not wait until you arrive at an island and realize you are locked out.

walkthrough.title: Player 4: Efficient Trading
walkthrough.text: Player four visits their market and trades yellow spices up to red. Then next turn, they move one tile to a second market where they have a post and trade red up to green. Player four is building a chain. Yellow to red at one island, red to green at the next. Each stop upgrades their spices closer to what the point cards require.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Fulfilling a Point Card
walkthrough.text: Player one has been trading efficiently and now has the right spices to fulfill the first point card. They sail to a port island and pay the required spices. Player one takes the point card and the three-point bonus token sitting on top. That is a great haul for the first point card of the game.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: The Third Mistake — Hoarding Yellow
walkthrough.text: Player three has been harvesting yellow spices on back-to-back turns. They now have eight yellow cubes in cargo. The problem is that none of the available point cards want yellow spices. They all require red, green, or brown. Player three is sitting on a pile of the lowest-value resource with no way to spend it quickly. Meanwhile, other players have been upgrading and scoring.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Upgrade Your Spices
walkthrough.text: Yellow spices are the raw material, not the finished product. Harvesting is useful for restocking, but sitting on a cargo hold full of yellow is a trap. Yellow cubes are worth zero points at end of game, and point cards almost always demand higher-value spices. Your goal should be to convert yellow into red, red into green, and green into brown as efficiently as possible. Trade early and trade often.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Recovery
walkthrough.text: Player three changes course. They sail to their one trading post and start converting yellow into red. Next turn, they will move to a nearby market, build a post, and convert red into green. It is not too late to catch up, but Player three has lost several turns compared to the others. The lesson is sinking in.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have walked through the key moments of an early game of Century: Eastern Wonders. You have seen how to harvest, build trading posts, trade at markets, and fulfill point cards. You also watched Player three learn three important lessons: do not waste spices on long moves, build posts before you need them, and upgrade your spices instead of hoarding yellow. You are ready to play on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Set Sail
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to spice types, actions, and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Set Sail
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for spices and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the biggest mistakes new players make. First, moving too many tiles and wasting spices on movement. Second, not building enough trading posts early, leaving you with nowhere to trade. Third, hoarding yellow spices instead of upgrading them. Fourth, ignoring the bonus tokens on the first two point cards. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the most common traps.

walkthrough.title: Build Your Trade Network Early
walkthrough.text: Your first few turns should focus on building trading posts, not scoring points. Think of posts as infrastructure. You need them to access markets, and the more you have, the more flexible your routes become. Aim to build two or three posts in the first three or four turns. Place them on markets that form a chain from low-value to high-value conversions. A network of yellow to red, then red to green, then green to brown gives you a clear path to fulfilling any point card.

walkthrough.title: Plan Short Efficient Routes
walkthrough.text: Movement costs add up fast. Every extra tile costs one spice, and those dropped spices become freebies for your opponents. The best players build trading posts close together so they can hop between markets in one or two moves. When you are deciding where to build, look at the board and find clusters of useful markets within one or two tiles of each other. A tight route that you can loop through repeatedly is far better than zig-zagging across the board.

walkthrough.title: Race for Bonus Tokens
walkthrough.text: The bonus tokens on the first two point card positions are worth three and one extra victory points. These are free points for whoever claims them first. When a card with a bonus is within reach, it is often worth going slightly out of your way to grab it. The three-point bonus especially can be the difference between winning and losing. Check the point card row every turn and ask yourself if you can fulfill one of the top two.

walkthrough.title: Unlock Player Board Bonuses
walkthrough.text: When you remove trading posts from your player board, you can unlock permanent bonuses. These bonuses trigger for the rest of the game, so getting them early is extremely valuable. Look at your player board and plan which posts to build to reveal bonuses as quickly as possible. Sometimes it is worth building a post on a less ideal market just to unlock a powerful player board bonus.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Point Card Row
walkthrough.text: Always keep an eye on which point cards are available and what spices they require. If two or three cards all want green and brown spices, that tells you where to focus your trading. If a new card appears that perfectly matches what you already have in cargo, pivot to grab it. The point card row constantly shifts as players claim cards and new ones appear, so reassess every turn.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Cargo Wisely
walkthrough.text: With a ten-cube limit, cargo space is precious. Do not fill up on yellow spices and leave no room for higher-value cubes. A good rule of thumb is to keep two or three slots open so you can pick up dropped spices or receive cubes from a good trade. Convert and spend spices regularly to keep your cargo lean and flexible. Sitting on ten cubes with nothing to do means you are wasting opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Awareness
walkthrough.text: The game ends when someone claims their fourth point card, and then the round finishes. Keep counting how many cards each opponent has claimed. When someone has three cards, the end is near. Make sure you have spices ready to fulfill one last card or at least convert your remaining cargo into non-yellow spices for the one-point-each end-game bonus. Getting caught with a cargo full of yellow on the last turn is a painful way to lose.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't waste spices on long movement — stay close early
Don't skip building trading posts — you need them to trade
Don't hoard yellow — upgrade to red/green/brown ASAP
Don't ignore bonus tokens on point card positions 1-2

walkthrough.title: Speed Strategy
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest approaches in Century: Eastern Wonders is the speed strategy. The idea is to fulfill point cards as fast as possible, triggering the game end before opponents can build their engines. Focus on cheap point cards that require fewer or lower-value spices. Build just enough trading posts to access one or two efficient conversions, then start delivering immediately. You sacrifice big-point cards for velocity, ending the game while others are still setting up.

walkthrough.title: Building the Speed Engine
walkthrough.text: To execute the speed strategy, build two or three trading posts near a port in the first few turns. Choose markets that convert yellow into the specific spices the cheapest point cards require. Keep your routes short, ideally one or two tiles between each stop. Harvest, trade, deliver, repeat. Every turn should be productive. If you can claim a card every three or four turns, you will trigger the end game before most opponents have their second card.

walkthrough.title: High-Value Card Strategy
walkthrough.text: The opposite approach targets the most expensive point cards worth the highest victory points. These cards demand rare spices like green and brown, so you need a deeper trade network. Build four or five trading posts forming a conversion chain from yellow all the way to brown. You will score fewer cards total, but each card is worth significantly more points. Pair this with player board bonuses to offset the slower pace.

walkthrough.title: Player Board Bonus Optimization
walkthrough.text: The player board bonuses are hidden point accelerators that many players overlook. Before the game starts, study your board and identify which patterns unlock the best bonuses. Then plan your early trading post placements to reveal those bonuses as quickly as possible. A bonus that grants a free spice upgrade every turn is worth multiple points over the course of the game. Optimizing your build order around bonuses gives you a compounding advantage that grows every turn.

walkthrough.title: Route Denial
walkthrough.text: In a two or three player game, you can use route denial to slow down opponents. When you build a trading post on a market island, it does not block others from using it. But you can claim the point cards they are working toward. Watch what spices your opponents are stockpiling and which cards they are eyeing. If you can pivot to claim that card first, you force them to waste turns pivoting to a new target. Timing is everything. Deny the card right before they can deliver.

walkthrough.title: Spice Trail Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Remember that every extra movement spice you drop becomes a gift for opponents. Advanced players use this knowledge in two ways. First, plan routes that minimize dropped spices by building posts close together. Second, be aware of where opponents' boats are. If you must drop spices, try to leave them on tiles far from your opponents' routes. Conversely, watch for spices other players drop and adjust your route to collect them for free fuel.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Game End
walkthrough.text: Controlling when the game ends is a powerful strategic lever. If you are ahead, accelerate the ending by claiming your fourth card. If you are behind, you want to delay by targeting high-value cards that take longer to fulfill. Count everyone's claimed cards constantly. When you have three cards and a competitor also has three, it becomes a race. Make sure you have the spices in cargo or one trade away from fulfilling that fourth card before your opponent does.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Cargo Optimization
walkthrough.text: In the final turns, every spice in your cargo matters. Yellow cubes are worth zero points, but red, green, and brown are each worth one. If you have turns remaining after claiming your last card, spend them upgrading your cargo. Trade yellow into red, red into green, and so on. Even converting three yellow into one green at a market nets you one point for free. A cargo of six non-yellow spices at game end is six bonus points, which can easily swing the outcome.

summary.title: Speed Strategy
bullets: Fulfill cheap point cards as fast as possible
Trigger game end before opponents build their engines
Sacrifice big-point cards for velocity
Aim to claim a card every 3-4 turns

bullets: Build 2-3 posts near a port in the first few turns

Keep routes to 1-2 tiles between stops
Harvest → trade → deliver → repeat every cycle

summary.title: Endgame Cargo Optimization
bullets: Yellow = 0 VP; red/green/brown = 1 VP each
Spend final turns upgrading cargo from yellow to higher
6 non-yellow spices at game end = 6 bonus VP
Even one conversion can swing the outcome

entries.definition: The lowest-value spice cube. Used as the basic raw material for trading up. Collected from harvest islands (two per visit). Worth zero victory points at end of game.
The second-tier spice cube. Obtained by trading yellow spices at market islands. Worth one victory point at end of game if remaining in cargo.
The third-tier spice cube. Obtained by trading red spices at market islands. Worth one victory point at end of game if remaining in cargo.
The highest-value spice cube. Obtained by trading green spices at market islands. Required by the most valuable point cards. Worth one victory point at end of game if remaining in cargo.

The four spice types ranked from lowest to highest: yellow, red, green, brown. Trading at markets converts lower-tier spices into higher-tier ones. Yellow is worth zero at end of game; all others are worth one point each.

An action taken on a market island where you have a trading post. Trade spices according to the market tile's conversion rate. May repeat the conversion multiple times per visit as long as you have spices to pay.
An action taken on a port island. Pay the required spices shown on a face-up point card to claim it. If the card is in position one or two and a bonus token remains, take the bonus token as well.
An action taken on a harvest island. Collect two yellow spice cubes from the supply at no cost. No trading post required. Useful for restocking raw materials.

An action taken on any market island. Remove the leftmost trading post from your player board and place it on the island. Cost scales based on how many posts you have built in that column. Required before you can use a market's conversion.

The first phase of every turn. Move your boat one sea tile for free. Each additional tile costs one spice of any type, dropped onto the tile you leave. You may pick up any spices on tiles you pass through for free.

A wooden token placed on a market island to grant permanent access to that market's conversion. Removed from your player board when built. Costs increase as you build more posts in each column. Building in specific patterns unlocks player board bonuses.

The cost to build a trading post depends on the column on your player board. First post in each column is free. Second costs one spice matching the island terrain. Third costs two spices, and so on. Plan build order to minimize total cost.

Permanent bonuses revealed when trading posts are removed from your player board in certain patterns. Bonuses may include free spice upgrades, extra spices when trading, or other lasting advantages. Unlocked bonuses apply for the rest of the game.

A card showing a required combination of spices and a victory point value. Claimed by taking the Port action and paying the listed spices. Four cards are always face up. When one is claimed, remaining cards slide left and a new card is dealt to the right.

Extra victory point tokens placed above the first and second point card positions. The first position has a three-point token, the second has a one-point token. Claimed along with the point card. Replaced when a new token is available.
Hexagonal tiles that form the modular game board. Boats move between adjacent sea tiles. Spices dropped during movement land on sea tiles and can be picked up by other players passing through.

An island on a sea tile with a market tile showing a spice conversion. Players must build a trading post here before they can trade. Each market island has a specific terrain type that determines trading post costs.
An island where players can fulfill point cards by paying the required spices. No trading post is needed to use a port island.
An island where players can collect two yellow spice cubes for free. No trading post is needed. Located on the edges of the board. A key source of raw materials throughout the game.
Your personal spice storage. Can hold a maximum of ten spice cubes at any time. Excess spices beyond the limit are lost. Manage cargo carefully to keep room for trades and pickups.
Your player token that moves across the sea tiles. Placed on an edge sea tile during setup. Moves one tile for free each turn, with extra tiles costing one spice each.

Spice cubes left on sea tiles as payment for extra movement. Any player who moves through a tile with dropped spices picks them all up for free. Can be strategically important — avoid giving opponents free resources.

Triggered when any player claims their fourth point card. The current round is finished so all players have equal turns. Then proceed to final scoring: point cards plus bonus tokens plus one point per non-yellow spice in cargo.

Total score equals victory points from point cards, plus bonus tokens, plus one point for each non-yellow spice remaining in cargo. Yellow spices are worth zero. Tiebreaker: most total spice cubes remaining in cargo.
